game_name: Romance of the Three Kingdoms II

danger_signals.visual_form: Province control map showing enemy-colored territories advancing toward player holdings
danger_signals.meaning: Immediate threat to player domains; military action required

danger_signals.visual_form: Reputation/loyalty meter decline for controlled officers
danger_signals.meaning: Risk of defection or betrayal within player's own forces

danger_signals.visual_form: Visual or UI alert indicating enemy communication in transit
danger_signals.meaning: Enemy coordination attempt; tactical vulnerability window

safety_signals.signal: Controlled provinces with high land value
safety_signals.visual_form: Visually distinct provinces colored in player faction color with enhanced visual indicators
safety_signals.meaning: Secure territory generating resources and stability

safety_signals.visual_form: Officer with Intelligence stat of 100 in advisory position
safety_signals.meaning: Accurate strategic predictions; reduced uncertainty in military/diplomatic planning

safety_signals.visual_form: Character portrait or stat display showing high Charm value
safety_signals.meaning: Improved recruitment and retention of new officers; stable power base

faction_indicators.color_identity: Player-assigned territory color (game engine assigns per faction)
faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Warlord character portrait and name displayed on controlled provinces
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Enemy warlord factions use contrasting colors on map; each scenario features 2-6 distinct warlord factions with visual differentiation
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Province borders and capital cities marked with controlling faction's color; territory clustering indicates allied or hostile bloc formation

faction_indicators.faction: Wei, Wu, and Shu (Three Kingdoms endgame factions)
faction_indicators.color_identity: Historically distinct faction colors (implied by historical novel structure)
faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Faction names correspond to historical kingdoms in Romance of the Three Kingdoms narrative
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Scenarios progress from multi-warlord fragmentation (AD 189-208) toward tripartite division (AD 215-220)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Geographic territorial blocks reflecting historical Three Kingdoms division of China

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Map displays 5+ independent warlord-controlled colored regions scattered across 41 provinces; high territorial fragmentation
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Chaos simulation: multiple simultaneous warlord expansion attempts; player must manage simultaneous threats from unpredictable factions

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Map shows reduced number of major territorial blocs; Cao Cao faction shown as militarily dominant force
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Cao Cao supremacy attempt creates unified threat vector; other warlords form defensive alliances or competition blocs

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Map displays three major colored regions corresponding to Wei, Wu, and Shu historical kingdoms; clear territorial tripartition
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Three-way cold war structure: player controls one kingdom faction and competes against two stable rival powers

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: High land value provinces display enhanced visual distinction or marker indicating resource generation capacity
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Developed provinces attract officer recruitment and support higher strategic decision success rates

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Reputation system meter shows low loyalty values for key officers; visual indicator of defection risk
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Officers may switch allegiance to rival warlords if loyalty drops below threshold; player loses command capability in contested provinces

environmental_storytelling.example: Six scenarios labeled with historical dates (AD 189, 194, 201, 208, 215, 220) and key historical events (Dong Zhuo seizes Luoyang; Rise of Wei, Wu, Shu). Each scenario title communicates the political crisis state of that era.

environmental_storytelling.technique: Territory clustering and natural geographic divisions
environmental_storytelling.example: 41 provinces arranged to reflect historical China geography; player observes natural geographic chokepoints and defensive positions that mirror historical military strategy

200+ unique officers available, many drawn from Romance of the Three Kingdoms novel. Officer appearance and stats reflect historical roles (e.g., high Intelligence officers serve as strategists/advisers)
Scenario selection determines which warlord player controls and their starting territory. Initial distribution reflects novel's historical setup, communicating relative power imbalance at each era

environmental_storytelling.technique: Special items and treasures as world-state markers
environmental_storytelling.example: Treasures and stat-boosting items function as rare strategic resources that convey scarcity and high-value objective positioning within territories

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Continues service; provides stat-based support in duels, planning, and province management
Risk of defection to rival warlords; reduced success rate in assigned tasks; may abandon player without warning
Provides perfectly accurate strategic predictions; eliminates uncertainty in military/diplomatic planning outcomes
Provides probabilistic predictions with accuracy inversely related to intelligence gap; creates strategic uncertainty
Victory achieved; game ends with player triumph
Expands aggressively; intercepts player communications; competes for officer recruitment
Becomes independent warlord; can ally with or oppose player based on relationship mechanics

notes: Romance of the Three Kingdoms II communicates world-state primarily through turn-based strategic map representation. Danger and safety are expressed via territory control visualization, officer loyalty/reputation meters, and scenario-based political context. Factions are distinguished by territory color and character roster composition rather than explicit visual sigils. The game's six scenarios function as narrative/political state markers, each representing a distinct historical crisis phase. Environmental storytelling relies heavily on historical grounding (dates, character names from novel, geographic authenticity) rather than dynamic visual manifestation. The reputation system creates dynamic danger signals through officer loyalty fluctuation. Officer stats (Intelligence, War Ability, Charm) drive behavioral differences that communicate strategic role differentiation.
